data.title: How did crypto enthusiast SBF invest in the most valuable companies of the AI ​​era?
data.description:
Anthropic is now arguably the most important AI company on the planet.

Its Claude model is deployed in the Pentagon, US intelligence agencies, and national laboratories, and is used by the US military for intelligence analysis and target selection in preparation for military strikes against Iran.

Its annualized revenue has soared from zero to $14 billion in less than three years. In February 2026, Anthropic completed a $30 billion Series G funding round, with a post-money valuation exceeding $380 billion. Amazon, Google, Nvidia, Microsoft—tech giants are lining up to pour money into it.

In the past few weeks, it has been engaged in a globally watched contest with the Pentagon over the weaponization of AI.

In the early funding history of this company, one name remains a topic of conversation: Sam Bankman-Fried. In April 2022, ChatGPT didn't even exist, and the AI ​​sector was far from its current hype. SBF, through his hedge fund Alameda Research, poured $500 million into Anthropic's Series B funding round, acquiring 86% of the entire round and approximately 8% of the equity. Seven months later, the FTX empire collapsed, and SBF became the protagonist of one of the biggest cryptocurrency fraud cases in history, sentenced to 25 years in prison. That $500 million was deposits from FTX customers. But if SBF hadn't been arrested, and if the money had been legally obtained, at today's valuation of $380 billion, an 8% stake would theoretically be worth over $30 billion. Turning $500 million into $30 billion—a return of over 60 times—is a record-breaking profit in the history of venture capital. A crypto fraudster currently serving time in federal prison almost made the most insane bet in AI investment history. How did SBF find Anthropic in 2022? Why did he dare to invest $500 million? And why did Anthropic accept the money? The answer lies within a circle called "Effective Altruism." A shared apartment, a movement, a check. In mid-2010s San Francisco, a group of people lived in the same type of shared apartment, attended the same type of gatherings, read the same type of papers, and believed in the same philosophy. This philosophy is called Effective Altruism (EA). Its core proposition is simple: charity should not be based on feeling, but on calculation. Every dollar should flow in the direction that mathematically "maximizes the good." And according to a significant branch of EA, the greatest existential risk facing humanity is not nuclear war, nor a plague, but out-of-control artificial intelligence. Dario Amodei is deeply involved in this community. He was the 43rd signatory to the Giving What We Can Pledge, pledging to donate at least 10% of his income. He became a fan of GiveWell as early as 2007 or 2008. He shared a house with two other people: Holden Karnofsky, co-founder of GiveWell and Open Philanthropy, and one of the most influential fundraisers in the EA movement; and Paul Christiano, a key researcher in AI alignment. At the time, Dario and Paul were both serving as technical advisors for Open Philanthropy. Later, Karnofsky married Dario's sister, Daniela. After their engagement, the couple lived with Dario for a time. In January 2025, Karnofsky quietly joined Anthropic as a "technical employee," responsible for security strategy. Anthropic hadn't even publicly announced the appointment when Fortune reporters discovered it. This is a close-knit social network. Amanda Askell, an early employee of Anthropic, is the ex-wife of William MacAskill, one of the founders of the EA movement. She was the 67th signatory to the GWWC, and her doctoral dissertation focused on a core issue in EA's philosophy: how to deal with infinity in ethics. Anthropic's most important governance body, the Long-Term Benefit Trust, theoretically wields significant control over the company. Three of its four members come directly from the EA system: Neil Buddy Shah, former managing director of GiveWell; Zach Robinson, CEO of the Center for Effective Altruism; and Kanika Bahl, CEO of Evidence Action, a long-time GiveWell beneficiary. The three biggest financial backers in the history of the EA movement were all early investors in Anthropic: Facebook co-founder Dustin Moskovitz, Skype co-founder Jaan Tallinn, and Sam Bankman-Fried. This is the real path SBF took to find Anthropic. It wasn't some brilliant investment insight, nor a forward-thinking judgment on the AI ​​field; it was simply a money cycle within an industry: EA's money flows to EA's projects, solving the problems defined by EAs. SBF subscribes to a more radical approach within EAs: "earning to give." He resigned from the Wall Street quantitative firm Jane Street to pursue cryptocurrency, publicly declaring that his goal wasn't personal wealth, but "altruism"—to first make as much money as possible and then invest it in areas that would have the greatest positive impact. Anthropic's mission, "safely developing powerful AI," is practically the standard prescription for EAs regarding the inherent risks of AI. In May 2021, Jaan Tallinn led Anthropic's Series A funding round, raising $124 million, with Moskovitz also participating. In April 2022, SBF took over as lead investor in the Series B round, writing a check for $500 million, representing 86% of the total $580 million raised. Other investors in the same round included Caroline Ellison, Nishad Singh, and James McClave of Jane Street. This list of investors speaks volumes. Caroline Ellison is the CEO of Alameda, Nishad Singh is the Engineering Director at FTX, and Jane Street is SBF's former employer. This $580 million Series B funding round actually came almost entirely from SBF and the pool of funds controlled by it. Red Flags and Compromises Dario Amodei wasn't stupid. In a later in-depth interview, he recalled that SBF seemed like someone "bullish on AI and concerned about security," which aligned well with Anthropic's direction. But then Dario made a crucial statement: he sensed "enough red flags." So he made a decision: take the money, but isolate it in the governance structure. SBF received non-voting shares and was excluded from the board of directors. Dario later described SBF's actions as "far, far, far more extreme and egregious than I had imagined," three "much more"s stacked together. This decision later proved extremely wise. But it also left a sharp question: if the warning signs were so numerous that isolation in the governance structure was necessary, why did they still take it? You could argue that the AI ​​funding environment in early 2022 was far less vibrant than it is today, and Anthropic needed substantial funds to build computing power; finding an investor willing to provide $500 million at once, regardless of their number of "red flags," was difficult. But there's a more subtle reason: in the operating logic of the EA (Expert Advisor) circle, the "cleanliness" of the funding source is never a priority. The key is the "effectiveness" of the funds—whether they can help you do more. SBF's entire wealth narrative is built on this foundation: making money is the means, doing good is the end, so the way you make money doesn't have to be so particular, as long as the final "good" output is large enough. This logic, in SBF's hands, took it to the extreme of crime, but at the moment he invested in Anthropic, it seemed like just a radical but not illegal philosophical choice. After the collapse: a dark comedy. The rest, as everyone in the crypto world knows, is history. In November 2022, CoinDesk exposed Alameda's balance sheet, and Changpeng Zhao announced the sale of FTT, triggering a run on FTX and causing the empire to collapse within nine days. SBF was arrested, extradited, and tried, and sentenced to 25 years in prison in March 2024. Anthropic's 8% stake, along with all its assets, was frozen in bankruptcy proceedings. One noteworthy episode from the trial, excluded by the court, occurred during the trial. SBF's defense lawyers attempted to use Anthropic's investment as evidence of "foresight," arguing, "Look, he didn't just squander money; he made an investment decision that multiplied its value several times over." Prosecutor Damian Williams responded firmly: whether these investments were profitable or not was completely irrelevant to the fraud charge. Even if you steal someone else's money to invest and make a profit, it's still stealing. The judge accepted the prosecution's argument, and Anthropic's name was excluded from the trial. The prosecution added insult to injury: Isn't FTX itself the best example of what not to do? Valued at $18 billion in 2021, $32 billion in 2022, and worthless today. Then came the liquidation auction. In March 2024, the first round of valuations reached $884 million. The largest buyer, Abu Dhabi's sovereign wealth fund Mubadala, invested $500 million, exactly the same amount SBF invested. The second largest buyer was Jane Street, the former employer of SBF and Caroline Ellison. Craig Falls, head of quantitative research at Jane Street, even personally invested $20 million. SBF's first job after graduating from MIT was as a trader at Jane Street, and now his former employer has bought back the shares purchased by a former employee with embezzled funds. A total of $1.34 billion was recovered in the two rounds. This money flowed into FTX's creditor compensation pool, becoming an important source of funds for victims to recover their deposits. What if the liquidation team hadn't sold? In February 2026, Anthropic completed a $30 billion Series G funding round, bringing its post-money valuation to $380 billion. Without considering dilution, that 8% theoretically went from $1.34 billion to $30 billion. The liquidation team, of course, didn't choose this path; their responsibility was to liquidate assets as quickly as possible to repay creditors. However, this numerical difference—$1.34 billion vs. a potential value exceeding $30 billion—is key to understanding why this story is still being discussed today. It is the biggest regret in the entire FTX bankruptcy case. The Collective Amnesia of EA Anthropic's size and influence today are undeniable, but an interesting phenomenon is that the company is systematically distancing itself from the EA movement. Its seven co-founders have pledged to donate 80% of their personal wealth, which, at current valuations, is worth approximately $38 billion. Nearly 30 Anthropic employees registered for the EA conference in San Francisco, more than double the combined attendance of OpenAI, Google DeepMind, xAI, and Meta Superintelligence Labs. But Daniela Amodei said in an interview with Wired, “I’m not an expert on effective altruism. I don’t agree with that term. My impression is that it’s a bit outdated.” The woman who said this has a husband who is one of the most influential funders in the EA campaign and has just joined her company. This stance of “taking EA’s money, using EA’s people, living in EA’s shared housing, but not acknowledging EA” became understandable after the SBF case. The collapse of FTX caused the reputation of the EA campaign to plummet. Anthropic needs to distance itself from this label, just as any smart company would cut ties when its brand is negatively associated. But the facts are there: Anthropic's founding logic stemmed from the core arguments within the EA (Expert Advisor) community regarding the inherent risks of AI; its early funding came almost entirely from funds within the EA network; and its governance structure is controlled by individuals within the EA system. Sam Bankman-Fried is currently in a federal prison. He won't be released until at least 2049. By then, he will be 57 years old. During his imprisonment, the AI ​​company he invested in with embezzled funds has grown to a valuation exceeding $380 billion and is engaged in a high-profile battle with the Pentagon over the weaponization of AI. Its founder has become a regular feature in The New York Times and on Capitol Hill. If all is legal, that $500 million bet could make SBF one of the most profitable venture capitalists of our time. SBF's "making money and donating money" and Anthropic's "safe development of AI" share the same underlying operating system, willing to bear unusual methods and risks for sufficiently large positive outcomes. SBF pushed this logic beyond the boundaries of crime. Anthropic operates on the safe side of this line, but its core proposition—"We must build the most powerful AI ourselves to ensure its safety"—is itself a grand, almost self-justifying gamble. They grew in the same soil. In that soil, Dario and SBF attended the same gatherings, believed in the same philosophy, and lived on different nodes of the same social network. One reached a $380 billion AI empire, the other ended up in federal prison. And the $500 million check that connected them remains the most bizarre page in Anthropic's history.

data.title: Iran War - Petrodollars - AI Giants: A New Trigger for Financial Crisis
data.description:
A war in the Middle East is precisely severing the "main artery" of the US financial system. On the surface, the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz appears to be merely a geopolitically triggered energy crisis; however, beneath the surface, it is disrupting a hidden lifeline that has supported the global capital market for decades—the petrodollar cycle. Even more devastatingly, across the Pacific in the United States, a massive powder keg is already hissing: the US private lending market is deteriorating at an accelerating pace, with consumer loan funds freezing redemptions, Wall Street giants urgently withdrawing debt issuances, and industry executives issuing public warnings. When the "Middle East funding disruption" collides with "Wall Street bleeding," two crisis threads are racing toward the same destination: a brewing US financial crisis. The mutual attacks between Israel and Iran are spreading to core energy facilities, shattering Wall Street's initial illusion that "the conflict will end in a few weeks." If the war with Iran continues to drag on, the crisis could erupt at the same point. Petrodollars: Middle Eastern Capital Behind the AI ​​Boom

Petrodollars: Middle Eastern Capital Behind the AI ​​Boom

To understand the underlying logic of this crisis, we must first understand the hidden gears in the global financial machine: the petrodollar cycle.

This concept was first proposed by economists el-Gamal and Jaffe:

Gulf oil-producing countries reinvest the enormous amounts of dollars they earn from selling oil into the international financial market. This not only prevented excessive inflation within Middle Eastern countries but also provided a continuous flow of liquidity to the global credit system. Since the oil crisis of 1973, this cycle has been the invisible cornerstone supporting the global financial system. History has long proven that once this lifeline is severed, the consequences will be catastrophic. The 1979 oil shock, coupled with the 1980 Iran-Iraq War, led Gulf states to frantically withdraw massive amounts of capital from the international banking system to fund the war. The result was a broken financial chain in Latin American countries, directly triggering the 1982 Latin American sovereign debt crisis—the first systemic financial crisis in history caused by the "disruption of the petrodollar." Today, the scale of this cycle has expanded to an astonishing degree. As of November 2025, the UAE's financial sector alone will hold assets totaling $1.4 trillion. More importantly, where has the Middle Eastern money gone? The answer is: Silicon Valley and Wall Street. The hottest AI sector right now, from Nvidia's chips and OpenAI's valuation to the rampant expansion of tech software loans in the private equity market, all have Middle Eastern backers. Last year, Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman visited the US, not only promising to increase investment in the US from $600 billion to $1 trillion, but also directly mentioning "AI chips and computing power" as the core. Subsequently, tech giants like Google, Oracle, and AMD signed $80 billion investment agreements with Saudi Arabia for "cutting-edge technology"; Saudi DataVolt even poured $20 billion into US AI data centers. In other words, Middle Eastern petrodollars were already deeply intertwined with the capital chain of the US AI industry. However, on February 28th, things stalled. The Strait of Hormuz was closed, and Iran laid mines. Fitch initially optimistically predicted a "mild impact from a one-month blockade," but reality delivered a harsh blow: not only was the strait permanently blocked, but key oil and gas infrastructure was also subjected to continuous bombing. The petrodollar, the lifeline flowing to the US capital market, is being tightly strangled by war. Private Equity Lending: A Powder Keg Already Burning. If the Middle East represents an external disruption, then within the US financial system, a crisis has already quietly taken shape. Before the outbreak of the Iran war, Wall Street's private equity lending market was already teetering on the brink. And what pushed it off the cliff was AI, which Middle Eastern capital was frantically chasing. In the past few years, private lending has experienced explosive growth. Their favorite activity was lending money to software-as-a-service (SaaS) companies acquired by private equity firms, earning exorbitant interest rate spreads. Data from UBS and Barclays reveals a terrifying concentration: business services and information technology combined account for a staggering 55% of private lending loan books. This was a perfect capital game until Anthropic's Claude emerged. AI began to disrupt the traditional business models of software companies with overwhelming force. SaaS company stock prices plummeted, and their cash flow to repay loans instantly became a pipe dream. With the underlying assets failing, the dominoes of private lending collapsed. See this "crash timeline": February 3: Private lending stocks crash, the "SaaS doomsday" shockwave spreads; February 21: Giant Blue Owl experiences unusual redemptions, seen by Wall Street as a "canary in the coal mine"; February 26: UBS warns of a record-breaking "chain of defaults" is imminent; March 5: BlackRock drastically cut a private loan from $100 to zero. March 6: BlackRock, the world's largest asset manager, imposed a "lockdown" (redemption restriction) on $26 billion in its funds. March 11: JPMorgan Chase lowered its collateral valuation, directly restricting lending to private equity firms. Currently, giants including Blackstone, BlackRock, and Morgan Stanley are facing a redemption run exceeding $10.1 billion, and can only barely repay 70%. The statements from industry leaders have further torn away the last veil of pretense. John Zito, co-president of Apollo, bluntly stated at an internal UBS meeting: "For an average small to medium-sized software company, recovering 20 to 40 cents from a loan is considered good." As for how long the redemption wave will last? "I don't know." JPMorgan Chase withdrew its $5.3 billion debt issuance to Qualtrics overnight. Bank of America analysts even detected a hint of the 2008 subprime crisis in the current atmosphere—"Back then, everyone said subprime lending was just a localized problem, until it devastated the entire world." The war timeline: The longer it drags on, the more dangerous it becomes. Given such a fragile financial foundation, why hasn't the US stock market collapsed yet? Because Wall Street elites are still clinging to the assumption that this is just a quick, localized conflict. Initially, Christopher Granville, Managing Director of Global Political Research at TS Lombard, predicted that the conflict would last a maximum of four to five weeks, and the market could withstand it. But this thin veil was completely torn apart on March 18th. That day, Israel directly bombed Iran's largest natural gas field; in retaliation, Iran retaliated that evening by using missiles to destroy Qatar's Ras Raffarin industrial city (a crucial energy hub). Granville revised his report overnight, upgrading his forecast from a "brief shock" to a "five-month energy turmoil similar to the 2022 Russia-Ukraine war." He pessimistically pointed out that the plan to reopen the Straits of Hormuz would most likely fail. Although Trump doesn't want high oil prices for the sake of the election, his statement that "closing the Strait is another country's problem" makes the possibility of the US intervening to quell the conflict as soon as possible extremely slim. Christopher Smart, managing partner of Arbroath Group, pointed out a disheartening reality: "Even if a miraculous ceasefire occurs tomorrow, the world already understands that the free flow of 20% of the world's oil depends on the whims of Iran's future government." UBS strategist Bhanu Baweja issued a final warning: Wall Street is accustomed to the infantile model of "the Fed intervening whenever policy goes wrong," but they are simply not prepared for a protracted war. If the conflict drags on until April, oil prices could fall below $150 per barrel. The convergence of three crises unfolds a roadmap of the financial crisis: **The source of the crisis is cut off:** The war disrupted the petrodollar cycle, draining Middle Eastern liquidity that was originally flowing to US AI and capital markets; **Internal bleeding:** The $1.8 trillion private lending market is facing a life-or-death reassessment under the impact of AI, with runs and defaults stampeding each other; **Protracted conflict:** The high oil prices resulting from a protracted war will severely limit the Federal Reserve's room for interest rate cuts, further exacerbating the already fragile credit environment. Crises never arrive in the way people anticipate. They always begin with a seemingly distant, localized conflict, following hidden capital flows, ultimately triggering an avalanche that leaves no one unscathed in the most prosperous financial centers.

data.title: AI Agent Economic Infrastructure Research Report (Part 1)
data.description:
Existing infrastructure is hostile to the Agent economy. Agents can already think and act autonomously at the "capability layer," but at the "economic layer," they remain locked into infrastructure designed for humans.

This article is an in-depth research report produced by OKX Ventures. Due to its length, it will be published in two parts: Part 1 focuses on the macro background, the x402 protocol, ERC-8004, and the Virtuals Protocol; Part 2 will focus on analyzing OpenClaw and overall industry trends. Stay tuned.

Summary

AI Agents are evolving from passive assistants to proactive economic participants.

This report, comprising six chapters, systematically outlines the core infrastructure stack, application ecosystem explosion, and industry evolution of the Agent Economy: At the macro level, it analyzes market forecasts and infrastructure gaps for Agentic Commerce; at the protocol level, it provides an in-depth analysis of three complementary protocols: x402, ERC-8004, and Virtuals Protocol; at the application level, it uses OpenClaw as a case study to examine the real-world implementation path of the Agent Economy; and finally, it offers a comprehensive industry assessment from the perspectives of competitive landscape, payment routes, security threats, and business models. x402 (the payment layer), jointly launched by Coinbase and Cloudflare, embeds stablecoin micropayments into the HTTP protocol layer. As of the end of 2025, it had processed over 100 million transactions, with an annualized payment volume of $600 million. ERC-8004 (Trust Layer), proposed by the Ethereum Foundation's dAI team in collaboration with MetaMask, Google, and Coinbase, provides AI Agents with three on-chain registry entries: identity, reputation, and verification. It was launched on the Ethereum mainnet on January 29, 2026. Virtuals Protocol (Business Layer) has built a complete Agent commercialization platform, enabling autonomous transactions between Agents through ACP. Over 18,000 Agents have been deployed, with an aGDP exceeding $479 million. OpenClaw (Application Layer), developed by Austrian developer Peter Steinberger, surpassed React within four months with over 250,000 GitHub stars, becoming the fastest-growing open-source project in GitHub history. It natively embeds AI into users' existing 20+ messaging platforms, triggering the Crypto community to spontaneously build on-chain economic infrastructure on it. It is the core sample for this report to observe the real interaction between Agents and on-chain protocols.

Chapter 1Macro Background

1.1Market Size Forecast

The Agentic Payment sector is in a phase of rapid expansion, and many institutions have optimistic forecasts for its market size:

1.2 Infrastructure Gap

The existing infrastructure is hostile to the Agent economy: OAuth requires human clicks, credit card forms require manual input, and data silos hinder autonomous access. Agents can already think and act autonomously at the "capability layer," but at the "economic layer," they are still locked into infrastructure designed for humans (identity/coordination/economic activities).

... Currently, two evolution paths have emerged: Centralized Compliance Path: Communication A2A + Tool Access MCP + Payment AP2/ACP (led by OpenAI and Stripe, pure Web2) Decentralized Permissionless Path: x402 + ERC-8004 / 8183 + ACP (Agent Collaboration Framework) alt="3nqGJYqVfUlHADHJbuzy1IXkuuYU9duIlQs6fNVz.png">

Note: As of March 2026, the average daily number of transactions has dropped significantly from its December peak, with infrastructure transactions experiencing the largest decline (>80%).

Chapter Twox402Protocol:AgentPayment Layer

x402 is an open-source payment protocol that, by reviving the HTTP 402 status code, allows any HTTP request to natively carry stablecoin payments, enabling AI Agents to conduct instant pay-per-use transactions.

Understanding x402 should not be viewed as just another payment protocol.

It represents a redesign of the basic unit of economic activity: from "registration → verification → authorization → use" to "payment → use". x402 = "Swift for agents". The current API economy relies on an implicit assumption: that humans are involved. The process of obtaining an API Key—registering → filling in email → verification → copying the Key → pasting it into the code—assuming human involvement at every step. This process won't work in an agent economy because AI agents cannot register, fill in forms, or manage keys themselves. x402 utilizes the HTTP 402 status code to achieve native stablecoin payments. Upon receiving a 402 response, the agent directly makes on-chain payments (USDC) and receives a receipt after payment.

2.1Protocol Overview and Workflow

Core Roles

    2.7 Key Risk Signals

    • Daily average transaction volume decreased from approximately 731,000 transactions in December 2025 to approximately 57,000 transactions in March 2026 (-92%), with an actual transaction size of approximately $14,000/day (according to Artemis, 95% of the peak daily average of $250,000 in December was Gamed)

    • Ecosystem market capitalization of $7 billion (LINK $6 billion + Virtuals $600 million), valuation is severely out of sync with actual usage

    • 3.1 Standard Overview and Core Distinctions

      In traditional interactions, because interactions between agents require establishing pre-existing trust relationships or relying on third-party institutions, they are often confined to the same ecosystem. In an open environment, how agents discover partners, view historical performance, and verify reliability are core issues.

      Important Distinction: ERC-8004 is not a token. It uses ERC-721 NFTs to internally represent agent identities, but the standard itself is about coordination and trust, does not carry economic value, and is not tradable.

      3.2 Three Major Registries

      Identity RegistryBased on ERC-721 + URIStorage, each Agent obtains an NFT identity identifier, associated with an agentURI pointing to a registration file (JSON), containing name, description, server endpoint (A2A/MCP/Web), x402 support status, etc. The URL can be stored in IPFS (decentralized, censorship-resistant), an HTTPS server (simple but centralized), or directly encoded on-chain (most decentralized but expensive).

      Reputation RegistryStandard interface for publishing and receiving feedback signals, supporting on-chain scoring and off-chain algorithms.

      An x402 proofOfPayment can be attached as an economic endorsement trust signal. Agents score each other, but to prevent score manipulation, ERC-8183 is required to further prove that there is genuine job interaction between agents. Validation Registry (Validation Registry) Introducing TEE (Trusted Execution Environment), PoS staking mechanism, and ZK (Zero-Knowledge Proof) to verify and authenticate the task output processed by the Agent: TEE: Verifies that the task is executed in a secure black box, and that the code and data have not been spied on or tampered with externally. PoS: Validators must stake assets to participate in the task; if they act maliciously, their stakes will be forfeited. ZK: Verifies the correctness of the Agent's reasoning process without needing to know its internal weights. 3.3 Development Milestones style="text-align:center">

      Supporters: ENS, EigenLayer, The Graph, Taiko. Approximately 1,000–2,000 developers have joined.

      However, Crapis itself acknowledges the current limitations of 8004: "8004 is essentially a registry." It issues IDs to agents and provides an evaluation mechanism, but it cannot guarantee the trustworthiness of agent behavior.

      ... True validation requires behavioral auditing (what the agent has done in the past), execution environment verification (evidence of operation within the TEE), and intent verification (whether the agent claims to do X and actually does X). The TEE portion of the Validation Registry is still under discussion with the community and is far from mature. In other words, 8004 is a necessary but not sufficient condition. It solves the problem of "who is this agent?", but not the problem of "is this agent trustworthy?". The latter requires a combination of 8004 + TEE + behavioral auditing, which has not yet been fully implemented. Of course, there is another underestimated area: in the human economy, the credit system is built on balance sheets and credit records—how much money you have, how much loan you have repaid in the past. Agents don't have these, but they do have behavioral data: how many tasks they have performed in the past, their success rate, their average response time, and whether they have been complained about. If behavioral data can be transformed into financial primitives, then 8004's reputation system wouldn't just be about good and bad reviews, but rather a credit score system within the agent world. An agent with a high reputation score can obtain a higher credit limit (more pre-authorized funds), lower transaction costs (due to lower risk), and priority in task allocation (employers prefer agents with good reputations). 8004's identity and reputation registry is merely the foundational data layer. Value creation lies in who can build agent credit assessments and financial services on top of this data layer—Agent loans, Agent insurance, Agent credit limits—that is, the entire financial services stack.

      3.4 Relationship with Other Protocols

      3.5 ERC-8183：Ethereum Standardization of ACP

      ERC-8183 is the Ethereum open standardized version of the Virtuals internal ACP protocol (released on March 10, 2026, currently in the Draft stage).

      ... The core primitive is the Job—an on-chain state machine (Open → Funded → Submitted → Completed/Rejected/Expired), with funds held in escrow via programmable Escrow, and automatic settlement after delivery quality is arbitrated by an independent Evaluator. It supports Hooks extension mechanisms (reputation thresholds, bidding, milestone payments, etc.). Key design: Each completed Job automatically generates interaction records and feeds them into the ERC-8004 Reputation Registry—analogous to "reviewing on Dianping (a Chinese review platform) after consumption, with the addition of third-party judges." This is the connecting point for the symbiotic loop between 8183 and 8004.

      Chapter 4Virtuals Protocol：AgentBusiness Layer

      4.1 Project Overview

      Virtuals Protocol is a decentralized full-stack AI Agent infrastructure that allows anyone to create, tokenize, co-own, and monetize autonomous AI agents on-chain. The project was initially founded in 2021 as PathDAO (a gaming guild), and transitioned to an AI Agent focus in early 2024. Currently, it is primarily deployed on Base and is expanding to Ethereum, Solana, and Ronin.

      Core Team: Founders Jansen Teng (former BCG advisor, undergraduate degree in Biotechnology and Business Management from Imperial College London) and Weekee Tiew (undergraduate degree in Biotechnology from Imperial College London + Master of Management from London Business School, PE/BCG background), headquartered in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, with approximately 38 team members. Funding History: PathDAO raised $16 million in seed round funding (led by DeFiance Capital and Beam).

      4.2Technical Architecture: Four Pillars

      Pillar One:GAMEFramework——How a SingleAgentMakes Decisions Internally

      GAME is the brain: it equips an Agent with goals, personality, perception abilities, and executable actions, allowing it to autonomously plan "what should I do next," and then breaks down tasks to internal Workers for execution. The entire process occurs within the boundaries of a single Agent.

      Core Architecture: Hierarchical Planning separates "what to think" from "how to do it"—The Task Generator (HLP) generates tasks and selects Workers based on the Agent's goals; each Worker (LLP) has a specific set of executable Functions; Functions execute specific API calls, on-chain transactions, data retrieval, and other operations. Supported Base Models: Llama 3.1 405B (default), Llama 3.3 70B, DeepSeek R1, DeepSeek V3—model-independent design. With the release of the OpenAI/Google Agent framework, GAME's differentiation is only one point: it is the only Agent framework that natively integrates an on-chain economic layer (ACP + VIRTUAL token). Pillar Two: ACP – Agent Commerce Protocol (ACP) is a standardized on-chain protocol that allows agents to discover, hire, negotiate, escrow funds, deliver, and settle accounts – all without human intervention.

      ACP Four-Phase State Machine

      Pillar Three:Butler—Super Entry Point for Users

      Butler is the consumer gateway of the ACP network—an Agent built on top of LLM, essentially an ACP protocol orchestrator, responsible for translating user natural language into on-chain multi-Agent collaborative workflows.

      ... Butler employs a two-layer architecture: the surface layer is an LLM chat interface (currently running on Gemini 3 Pro); the underlying layer is the ACP protocol orchestrator, executing the complete Agent discovery → quote confirmation → Escrow locking → task routing → delivery verification → fund release process. Users see the chat interface, but Butler handles contract scheduling. Butler Pro Mode explicitly separates planning and execution: Planning phase → Review phase (users can optimize the plan) → Execution phase (users can independently orchestrate the entire process). Built-in capabilities include Token Swap, DCA (Distributed Direct Investment), perpetual contracts, and Fund of Funds.

      Pillar Four: Launch Platform——AgentWall Street

      A three-tier launch system covers the complete lifecycle of Agent projects from 0→1→100:

      Titan's first projects: XMAQUINA ($DEUS, DAO holds equity in embodied intelligence companies such as Figure AI, $60 million FDV), Fabric Foundation ($ROBO, collaborating with OpenMind on the robotics economy)

      aGDP Growth Trajectory

      aGDP Quality Issues—Three Warning Signs:

      1. The $3 billion target is based on the following assumptions: it requires a 6.4x growth from $470 million to $3 billion. If speculation dominates aGDP, the target essentially bets on the Agent Token market hype rather than organic growth in the Agent economy. ...

      4.4 Token Economic Model

      $VIRTUAL Quadruple Value Capture Mechanism

      ACP Tax Structure: User Payment 100% → Agent Wallet 90% (withdrawable or can be used to hire other agents, composite on-chain aGDP) + Treasury 10% (of which 1% flows into G.A.M.E Treasury) → Treasury revenue continuously buys back Agents.

4.5 Ecological Data Overview

Benchmark Agent Case

4.6 Competitive Landscape and Moats

Moat Tiers (From Strongest to Weakest)

  • Network Effect + Token Flywheel (Strongest): 18,000+ Agents + 650,000+ Holders constitute a two-sided market. Each Agent is forced to pair with VIRTUAL, creating a positive feedback loop. This is something open-source frameworks cannot replicate—LangChain does not have a native inter-Agent economic settlement layer.
